details.text: Derivative: الْعَصْرِ – عَصْر comes from the verb عَصَرَ which means to press, wring and squeeze and is used for juice or clothes or figuratively like to squeeze out money from someone. It can refer to a time which is running out. It is the last part of the day when daytime is running out. It is related to the word عَصِير (‘Aseer) which is juice or something else that is squeezed out and ‘Asr is like time that has been squeezed and is escaping from your hands. الْعَصْرِ can therefore carry the implication of urgency in terms of the time that is slipping away from us. It has been said that it refers to the time of ‘Asr before sunset and also to the ‘Asr prayer itself. It has also been said to refer to a period of time or the passage of time, which could be one day to another through morning to night, or the whole passage of time itself in terms of passing generations, nations, ages and eras i.e., the history of humankind.

وَالْعَصْرِ

- The وَ is وَاوُ القَسْم (Waw ul-Qasm – The Waw of making an oath). It has been said that among other matters that the purpose of the oath is to make someone acknowledge the grandeur or magnificent nature of something and here the oath is being sworn by time. The oath is also used to emphasise and can demonstrate anger.
- When an oath is being made it usually has two components. The matter being sworn upon (Al-Muqsam ‘Alaihi) and the subject of the oath itself (Al-Muqsam Bihi). In Arabic rhetoric the matter being sworn upon (or the object) is a proof for the subject i.e., الْعَصْرِ (Al-‘Asr - Time) is an evidence and proof for the following statement and subject of the oath “No doubt, the human is in loss”.
- The object of the oath (i.e., that which is sworn upon known as Al-Muqsam ‘Alaihi) can also be a witness for the subject of the oath (i.e., the oath itself known as Al-Muqsam Bihi) i.e., الْعَصْرِ (Al-‘Asr - Time) is a witness over the human being in loss.

Derivative: الْإِنسَانَ (Al-Insaan – The human being) has been said to have come from different roots. One is that it comes from the verb نَسِيَ (Nasiya) which means “to forget” and it is linked to the human nature to forget things. الْإِنسَانَ (Al-Insaan) is اِسْمُ الجِنْس (Ism ul-Jins) which means that it is the name given for the category of human beings as a whole just as it is used to refer to the individual human being. The impact of this word is that it addresses every single human being individually whilst also addressing them as a whole.

Derivative: خُسْرٍ (Khusr) means loss and is from خَسِرَ. It was used in reference to trade, making a loss and even losing your capital investment. It is the opposite of “profit” (رِبْح – Ribh). There are two other words from the same root with similar meanings used in the Qur’an: خَسَارَة (Khasaarah) and خُسْرَان (Khusraan). خَسَارَة (Khasaarah) refers to a loss on top of a loss. خُسْرَان (Khusraan) is in the صِيغَة المُبَالَغَة (hyperbolised and empowered form) and it refers to an incredible, unimaginable and ultimate loss. The خُسْرٍ Khusr here refers to the loss in this life and the next.

Derivative: إِنَّ is used in the Arabic language not just for emphasis with the meaning of “Indeed” or “Verily” but to address those who are in doubt about what you are saying. إِنَّ (Inna) is therefore used to remove the doubt.

إِنَّ الْإِنسَانَ لَفِي خُسْرٍ

- A statement in Arabic can be empowered by employing certain tools. This statement grammatically speaking is the strongest that it can possibly be. Firstly, this is a جُمْلَة اِسْمِيَة (Jumlah Ismiyah – Noun-based sentence) as opposed to a verb-based sentence. In Arabic usage the noun-based sentence is stronger and more emphatic than the verbal sentence. The statement begins with إِنَّ (Inna) which is حَرْف التَّوْكِيد (Harf At-Tawkeed) which is used to provide emphasis. The third source of emphasis comes from the use of أَلِف لَام (ال) in the word الْإِنسَانَ (Al-Insaan) and implies every single human being thus adding a degree of emphasis. The fourth source of emphasis comes from the use ofلَام التَّوْكِيد (Laam At-Tawkeed) which is attached to فِي in لَفِي and is used for the purpose of adding emphasis. In addition, the use of the word فِي (in) implies that the person is immersed in Khusr (loss) which makes it more emphatic. The use of the noun خُسْرٍ (Khusr – loss) instead of a verb also adds power and emphasis to the statement. Even the use of the Tanween ٍ in خُسْرٍ (Khusrin) has been argued to establish that it is great in magnitude i.e., an incredible loss. Finally, this is the answer to an oath and the oath itself adds emphasis to it.

Derivative: عَمِلُوا is from عَمِلَ – عَمَلٌ which is close in meaning to فَعَلَ – فِعْلٌ. عَمَلٌ (‘Amal) refers to a conscious action while فِعْلٌ (Fi’l) can refer to a subconscious action. For example, looking and listening are acts where a conscious intent is involved as opposed to seeing and hearing.

Derivative: تَوَاصَوْاis from وَصِيَة which literally means a will or to leave parting words i.e., it relates to something which will benefit others after you, be of value to them and carries an element of urgency, care and concern. تَوَاصَوْا comes from the word تَواصٍ from the تَفَاعُل form which implies mutuality. It means to advise, enjoin, urge and exhort one another for something which is beneficial.

Derivative: الصَّبْرِ – The meaning of صَبْر (Sabr) includes patience, perseverance, commitment, constancy and steadfastness. Maintaining the same level of obedience and ease and difficulty is the manifestation of Sabr.

Derivative: الصَّالِحَاتِ here is an adjective that comes from the word صَلُحَ (Saluha) which means to reconcile and rectify. Because الصَّالِحَاتِ is an adjective it requires a noun to describe. Here الأَعْمال (actions or deeds) is omitted but implied because of the prior use of عَمِلُوا “They did”. It literally refers to something that corrects something and makes it upright and is understood to refer to righteous acts i.e., those which are in accordance with what Allah has commanded.

Derivative: الْحَقِّ – Al-Haqq literally means the truth and is used linguistically to refer to something that you know for certain is true. It is something that can be proven by evidence. It is also used to refer to an obligation or duty that is due upon you or a right that is due. It has been interpreted to refer here to what Islam has commanded to be undertaken, applied and established, to Tawhid, the correct beliefs, actions of obedience and abandoning the prohibitions, to the Qur’an, to speaking the truth and the acts of commanding the Ma’ruf (good) and forbidding the Munkar (evil).

إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالْحَقِّ وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ

- “Except (إِلَّا) those who (الَّذِينَ) have believed (آمَنُوا) and done righteous acts (وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ) and enjoined each other with the truth (وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالْحَقِّ) and enjoined each other with patience and perseverance (وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ)”. This is the exception to the previous Verse: “إِنَّ الْإِنسَانَ لَفِي خُسْرٍ” (Indeed, the human being is surely in (a state of) loss).
- The وَاو (Waw - وَ) “and” between each of the statements indicates that each one is required to meet the conditions of the exception i.e., the four conditions of believing, righteous actions, enjoining each other with the truth and enjoining each other with patience or perseverance.
- Enjoining one another with the truth (وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالْحَقِّ) and enjoining one another with patience and perseverance (وَتَوَاصَوْا بِالصَّبْرِ) both fall under عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحَاتِ “righteous deeds” and as such they have been specifically highlighted due to their significance.

- The preposition of بِ can be understood with different implications in relation to الصَّبْرِ “The patient perseverance”. The بِ can be understood to imply “patiently” i.e., they enjoin one another with patience بِالصَّبْرِ referring to the manner of enjoining. Or it can mean “to patience” i.e., enjoin one another to persevere and have patience.

- “Enjoining one another with patience and perseverance” has been interpreted to mean enjoining one another to be steadfast in being obedient to Allah, undertaking the Islamic obligations and to not fall into temptation and disobedience to Allah. It also means enjoining one another to remain steadfast in inviting to Islam, reminding people of Islam, commanding the good (Al-Ma’ruf) and forbidding the evil (Al-Munkar) and to persevere patiently through the harm and trials that are faced in that undertaking.

- بِالْحَقِّ – The preposition of بِ can be understood with three implications in relation to الْحَقِّ “The truth”. بِ can be an adverb which would make the meaning of بِالْحَقِّ “Truthfully” i.e., they advise and exhort in a truthful manner. بِ can also mean “with or by” where بِالْحَقِّ would mean “with the truth” i.e., by the means of the Qur’an, Sunnah and revelation. The third meaning of بِ is “to” where بِالْحَقِّ would mean “to the truth”.

الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا “Those who believed”. In respect to the difference between the use of the verb in the statement الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا “those who have believed” and the use of the noun المُؤْمِنُونَ “The believers” is that a verb is temporary and unstable whilst a noun is fixed and stable.
